All right, you're back. Lesson number one, here's what we're going to do. I want to stop by really explaining to you exactly how the beliefs work, how they end up sabotage your success and put you in this box, so to speak, where you keep trying less and less new things until you end up living this sort of average mediocre life that has no excitement, no passion and also very little money. It starts with what I call a cycle of beliefs and just so you know, I did not invent this content. I always give credit with credits, though. This is Dr. Richard Bannler who really highlighted this stuff to me, but I think I can show it to you, I teach it to you in a way that will really resonate with you, especially when it comes to taking action as a salesperson, as an entrepreneur. So that's my gift to you from Dr. Bannler. Okay? So, across the graphic here, we have what's called our cycle of belief and it starts with what's called pure potential, PP, right? And what that basically means is that we're all born with pure unlimited potential. Now, obviously in some extreme cases, people with Delta are very bad deck of cards, tough deck of cards, right? They have some real handicaps, so I'm not talking about that. And I'm also not talking about the extreme example of a Roger Federer or a Kobe Bryant or a Michael Jordan, right? Of course, people have certain inborn talents that are theirs. They own them at the highest level. But remember, those people also had empowering beliefs and worked to get to that level. But of course, some people have incredible talents, but all things being equal, remember that when we're born, we basically have this pure potential, what? Perfect beings of blank slate. Then comes the day two of our lives, right? For our parents not saying things to us about, you're good at this, you're not good at that. We experience things ourselves that we don't do well at or do well at, right? So watch. You next have your actions that you take. So watch how it works. Pure potential. We have an arrow going across now to the actions that we take. And what that A represents is all the things that we do throughout our life. For example, when I was very young, I was in kindergarten and I tried to draw a bowl of fruit. Seriously, me put down a bowl of fruit. She said, okay, everybody, everyone take that, your crayons and draw this bowl. And I start the drawing. I was having fun. I thought I was doing pretty good. I looked to my right and the girl next to me, she's like, it's like a photograph. Like, oh my God. I'm like, uh-oh, something's wrong here. Mrs. Roskin loved the teacher, right? You know, pump lady, right? She walks over and she goes to this other girl. Oh my God, Ginny. What a buddy young artist you are. How talented and Ginny is, fairly beaming. Oh, so great, right? And she comes over to me, she goes, well, Jordan, I guess you're not a Picasso. I would kiss that career goodbye. And I'm like, uh, and my heart sinks and I tried to nervously laugh, right? She was just stick to being a speaker and a mathematician because I was really advanced with math, even in kindergarten and speaking. It couldn't shut me up. I mean, right then and there, in that moment, she said that to me and it inserted a belief in my head. And to this day, you probably know, if you watch me draw on a page, I can't even draw a straight line. Now, let me ask you an honest question. Do you think I'm really missing an art gene? I mean, frankly, I've seen some things that pass as art. It's like a dot on a white page. Man's struggle man. It's a fricking dot and they sell from, I mean, come on. There's all types of art, art is about self expression. There's no right or wrong. Yes, I ain't Rembrandt. Okay. But what happened was, is those words stung me. So what happens is, watch, so I took an action, I got a what? A very negative result. And as a result of that, I ended up forming a limiting belief that I'm not really good at art. I'm not meant to do that, to be an artist and so forth. So now watch what happens again. Let's move the arrow back up, back to all pure potential. Uh-uh. I just had some potential cut off. You see, next time I go to draw something, I'm nervous. I'm shying away from it because of that bad feeling. And by the way, also, I'm not taking any art classes. I'm not going to try to peach myself because innate, we really don't try to do things that we're not good at or we don't think that we're good at. So next time I try and watch, I take another action in that art arena. How's my result? It's even worse because I cut my potential off now. So I get an even more negative result. I look at what, I'm like, that's so terrible. I'm screwed. What happens now? Right? That result cuts off more potential and on and on the cycle goes until you become Jordan Belfort, the worst artist in the world as I am today. You get it? Now, here's the deal. Many years later in life, I was watching cable TV and I saw this guy, he had like an afro and he was drawing, you know, this is your world. This is your picture. He did this amazing thing with teaching people how to draw or paint in like five seconds flat. And I'm like, damn, I can do this. And I started almost laughing to myself saying, I can't believe I allowed one individual and here's the funny part. They weren't even ill meaning. They were just, it was a passing comment, but these passing comments enter our minds out and go right to our unconscious mind and we don't have a so-called spyware to block it. People say things to us slips through the radar of the conscious mind, goes right to the unconscious mind and just like that, a limiting belief is formed and you cut off your potential. Get it? Now let's reverse this. The same thing holds true for an empowering belief. Let's see how one of those gets formed. We'll use me again as an example. I think I was in probably first or second grade and the teacher said, okay, everyone's going to come up and do some public speaking, right? Do a speech. So I'm like, oh, I'll get up and give a speech. I always love to talk. The words flowed out of me effortlessly since I remember three years old, seriously, four years old. So I get in front of the class, no preparation required, right? And I just thought giving this speech, I forgot what it was about the presidents and I watched these kids like in awe because even then I had a pretty natural ability to just speak publicly and keep people hanging on my every word. So what happened? When it was done, I walked off beaming everyone's like, hey, she's like, oh, Jordan, you're so talented, right? And I'm like, oh, great. I was like, when I told my mommy and my daddy, like, oh, you're so talented. That's how a powering belief gets formed. So here's what happened. Let's go back to the graph. So I have this pure potential, right? And I have a natural get but watch. I take my first action. What type of result do I get? I get a positive result. That positive result makes me feel good. So that increases my potential because now when I'm going to do it again, I'm feeling good. I'm in an empowered state. In fact, I'll probably even do some studying about how to speak publicly or how to make amazing speeches because it makes me feel good inside. You get it? As human beings, we tend to move towards things that give us pleasure. That includes the activities that we're good at. And now watch what happens. We go around and around. And each time I give another speech or speak out in front of a crowd, I keep getting better and better. And I get more positive feedback. I believe go stronger and stronger until finally it becomes a conviction, an unshackable belief that I am one of the best speakers at the one of the greatest communicators of all time. Now here's a question. Is that really true? Yes or no? Am I one of the greatest communicators of all time? Well I appreciate you answering yes and I kind of think so myself but here's the reality. It's just a belief. It's an opinion, a very strong one at that and also one that I could probably back up in so many but it's just a belief. It's not a truth. It's not a fact of life. It's debatable. Hmm. You don't think so. Thanks a lot. No, you're happy to be right by the way. Here's the deal. That's just an opinion as positive as I am that I'm right about that. And maybe you were pretty sure too but you got it though. In other words, it's just a belief. It's not a fact of life and that's the power that our beliefs have over us. They sit just below the surface and they disguise themselves as the facts of life. And by the way, once they're there, guess what? They will actually cause your brain to scour the world around you and they'll delete out anything that runs counter to that belief and they'll focus on only what's good. You get it? In other words, once you believe something, you can actually delete that all the stuff that doesn't really drive with that belief and we focus on the things that do and we just run that movie over again and again. It's wired with politics nowadays, right? People get into echo chambers and they become more and more extreme in one viewpoint because over time our opinions, our beliefs tend to reinforce themselves. You get it? I mean, this stuff is so powerful. I want to go to a quiz now but before I do, I just got to tell you one more quick story here to really drive this point home in terms of how insidious beliefs are, how they sit below the surface, scour the environment for what is true, delete out what they think is not true and then even change things that actually aren't true. They don't really fit the belief. They'll alter their perception to make it true. It's totally bizarre. Here's the exercise I do. For instance, if I say to you right now, everyone search around the room and look for blue. Everyone look for blue. Just do it. Look around for blue. Look for blue. Look for blue. Keep looking around the room. Just search the room for blue. Keep searching the room for blue. Everybody close your eyes. With your eyes closed, I want you to tell me everything in the room that's yellow. Now open up your eyes and search for yellow. Search for yellow. Search for yellow. Search for yellow. Keep searching. Not a lot of yellow here, right? Okay. And anybody here want to point out who? Anyone? Anyone? Now who sees more yellow now than they did before, by the way? It's not much yellow here, right? Okay. Who sees some yellow? Point to yellow. What do you see? The light. The light. The light's yellow. Good. What else is yellow? What's that? Can I see? Okay. Is that green? Okay. Is that green? See, that's green, okay? Perfect example. The reason I chose a color, this happens every time. Your brain will distort. You create shit. You make shit up to be right. If there was more yellow, it's amazing. It's very hardly any yellow here, right? But if there was some yellow here, that's yellow, that's yellow. And as soon as you're around yellow, you'd say, that's yellow. It's green. This is what the brain does. It makes shit up. You know the old adage, you look for and you shall find, well, in this case, it's look for, you shall invent. In other words, when you have a limiting belief, it will scour the environment for everything it believes that fits the belief, delete out whatever's counter to the belief. And then even if there's things that don't actually fall into the box, your brain will trick you into thinking that they do. That's how powerful it is. Conversely, if I do the whole look for red, look for red exercise, it's a close your eyes now and tell me everything in the room that's brown. They all start to laugh. They can't think of one thing that was brown. Why? Because when you're looking for red, that's all you'll find. You'll delete out what's not red and start making stuff up when it gets thin. Pretty powerful stuff, right? So with that, let's stop, go to a quiz and here's the deal. As soon as you're done, please meet me in the next lesson. I have a demonstration for you that's really going to lock this stuff in and step by step, we're going to identify your limiting beliefs. I'm going to show you exactly how to root them out, replace them with empowering beliefs and you by friend shall soar. I'll see you soon.
